summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Gillian Tett's Financial Times article that defends Chicago's predictive policing model as a 'neutral tool.' O'Neil dismantles the weak statistical evidence Tett uses to claim the program reduced murder rates, noting that correlation doesn't prove causation—as demonstrated by NYC's murder rates not rising when Stop & Frisk declined. More fundamentally, O'Neil argues that no algorithm trained on human behavior can be neutral because it inherently codifies historical biases, such as the documented racial disparity in marijuana arrests despite equal usage rates across races. She concludes that if even sophisticated commentators like Tett can be fooled by claims of algorithmic neutrality, there's much work to be done in public understanding.
key_points: Tett's evidence for predictive policing success is merely correlative—the small uptick in murders after the program ended could be statistical noise
NYC's Stop & Frisk experience disproves simple cause-effect claims: murder rates didn't spike when the practice was reduced
Algorithms trained on historical human behavior necessarily encode and perpetuate the biases embedded in that history
Racial disparities in arrest rates (like 4x higher marijuana arrests for Black Americans despite equal usage) get treated as 'neutral facts' by such models
If predictive models had been used for college admissions in 1870, they would have perpetuated the 0.7% female enrollment rate
money_quotes: No, Gillian Tett, there is no such thing as a neutral tool.
No algorithm focused on human behavior is neutral.
Anything which is trained on historical human behavior embeds and codifies historical and cultural practices.
Put it another way: if we allowed a model to be used for college admissions in 1870, we'd still have 0.7% of women going to college. Thank goodness we didn't have big data back then!
This is very scary to me, when even Gillian Tett, who famously predicted the financial crisis in 2006, can be fooled.
Here's the thing, it's really hard to actually know why murder rates go up and down.
key_insight: Algorithms trained on historical data don't neutrally predict the future—they perpetuate and legitimize the biases of the past.
